human_original_text(type1): Evolving graphs arise in problems where interrelations between data change over time. We present a breadth first search (BFS) algorithm for evolving graphs that computes the most direct influences between nodes at two different times. Using simple examples, we show that naive unfoldings of adjacency matrices miscount the number of temporal paths. By mapping an evolving graph to an adjacency matrix of an equivalent static graph, we prove that our generalization of the BFS algorithm correctly accounts for paths that traverse both space and time. Finally, we demonstrate how the BFS over evolving graphs can be applied to mine citation networks.

Our approach exploits the fact that a node can influence another node if and only if it is adjacent to the other node. We introduce a breadth-first search (BFS) algorithm for evolving graphs that calculates the most direct influences between nodes at two distinct points in time. By mapping an evolving graph to the adjacency matrix of an equivalent static graph, we demonstrate that our generalization of the BFS algorithm correctly accounts for paths that traverse both space and time. human_original_text(type1): The source code suggestions provided by current IDEs are mostly dependent on static type learning. These suggestions often end up proposing irrelevant suggestions for a peculiar context. Recently, deep learning-based approaches have shown great potential in the modeling of source code for various software engineering tasks. However, these techniques lack adequate generalization and resistance to acclimate the use of such models in a real-world software development environment. This letter presents DeepVS, an end-to-end deep neural code completion tool that learns from existing codebases by exploiting the bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (BiGRU) neural net. The proposed tool is capable of providing source code suggestions instantly in an IDE by using pre-trained BiGRU neural net. The evaluation of this work is two-fold, quantitative and qualitative. Through extensive evaluation on ten real-world open-source software systems, the proposed method shows significant performance enhancement and its practicality. Moreover, the results also suggest that DeepVS tool is capable of suggesting zero-day (unseen) code tokens by learning coding patterns from real-world software systems.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: Current IDEs mostly rely on static type inference to provide source code suggestions. This approach often results in irrelevant suggestions for a specific context. To address this limitation, deep learning-based approaches to type inference have been proposed. These approaches make the type inference process more dynamic and can take advantage of contextual information. However, deep learning-based approaches do not have access to information about the state of the program, which makes it difficult to generate accurate suggestions. This paper proposes a new approach to improve the performance of IDEs. Our approach uses contextual information to learn type information in the absence of static type information. We also present an approach to generate contextually relevant suggestions in an IDE. Our approach is based on a context-aware type inference method that is integrated into an IDE.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: Current IDEs provide code completion suggestions based mostly on static types. These suggestions are frequently irrelevant to the context. Recently, deep learning-based approaches have been proposed for code completion, which have shown promise. However, these approaches are often limited by the size of the training dataset, as they require a large number of labeled examples to learn the relationship between code snippets. We propose a novel approach to code completion that uses a novel dataset of human-generated code snippets. Our dataset is constructed by asking human participants to write code snippets for a given task. This dataset allows us to learn a more comprehensive representation of code snippets, which in turn leads to better code completion suggestions. We evaluate our approach on two standard benchmarks for code completion. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on both benchmarks, suggesting that our approach is able to generate more relevant and informative code completion suggestions. In addition to evaluating our approach on standard benchmarks, we also conduct a user study to compare our approach with a state-of-the-art deep learning-based approach. Our results show that our approach is able to generate more relevant and informative code completion suggestions, which leads to faster and more efficient code development.

human_original_text(type1): Modern applications significantly enhance user experience by adapting to each user's individual condition andor preferences. While this adaptation can greatly improve utility or be essential for the application to work (e.g., for ride-sharing applications), the exposure of user data to the application presents a significant privacy threat to the users, even when the traces are anonymized, since the statistical matching of an anonymized trace to prior user behavior can identify a user and their habits. Because of the current and growing algorithmic and computational capabilities of adversaries, provable privacy guarantees as a function of the degree of anonymization and obfuscation of the traces are necessary. Our previous work has established the requirements on anonymization and obfuscation in the case that data traces are independent between users. However, the data traces of different users will be dependent in many applications, and an adversary can potentially exploit such. In this paper, we consider the impact of correlation between user traces on their privacy. First, we demonstrate that the adversary can readily identify the association graph, revealing which user data traces are correlated. Next, we demonstrate that the adversary can use this association graph to break user privacy with significantly shorter traces than in the case when traces are independent between users, and that independent obfuscation of the data traces is often insufficient to remedy such. Finally, we discuss how the users can employ dependency in their obfuscation to improve their privacy.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: Modern applications improve user experience by customizing to each user's unique state and or preferences. While this customization can enhance utility or be vital to the application's function, it also increases the system's complexity. This is especially true in mobile devices, where the data required to adapt the system to a specific user is frequently saved on the device itself. Furthermore, as applications become more complex, they gather more and more data about the user and the context of their activities. This raises the amount of data needed for the system to be able to adapt. For example, in the context of a medical device, the data collected may include the patient's vital signs, blood test results, medical history, medication history, and personal preferences. Collecting and utilizing this data in a variety of ways is challenging. For instance, a physician may want to know if the patient is receiving any special treatments that could influence how they respond to the medical device. To obtain this information, the physician may need a second device, such as a personal computer, that can communicate with the medical device. The physician may also have to manually input the data collected by the medical device into the personal computer. This process can be time-consuming and tedious. Additionally, it is not feasible for the physician to keep track of all the data required to adapt the device to a specific patient because the data is saved on the device itself.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: Cutting-edge applications improve the user experience by tailoring it to each user's particular situation and inclination. While this personalization can significantly enhance utility or be essential for the application to function at all, it also enables applications to utilize personal data in potentially undesirable ways. To guarantee that users' privacy is protected, applications must be made to consider users' privacy preferences. In this paper, we present a mechanism that supports privacy preferences for context-aware applications. Our mechanism enables users to specify privacy preferences for the context data that are used by context-aware applications. It is designed to be general, allowing users to express their preferences regarding the use of context data in a flexible and expressive manner. Furthermore, it supports the expression of not only general preferences, but also more specific preferences, such as those that apply to particular context-aware applications or that are dependent on the current user context. To evaluate our mechanism, we conducted a user study in which 20 participants were asked to express privacy preferences using the mechanism. The findings indicate that our mechanism is simple to use and that users can express a wide range of privacy preferences.

human_original_text(type1): This paper describes the NTNU ASR system participating in the Interspeech 2020 Non-Native Children's Speech ASR Challenge supported by the SIG-CHILD group of ISCA. This ASR shared task is made much more challenging due to the coexisting diversity of non-native and children speaking characteristics. In the setting of closed-track evaluation, all participants were restricted to develop their systems merely based on the speech and text corpora provided by the organizer. To work around this under-resourced issue, we built our ASR system on top of CNN-TDNNF-based acoustic models, meanwhile harnessing the synergistic power of various data augmentation strategies, including both utterance- and word-level speed perturbation and spectrogram augmentation, alongside a simple yet effective data-cleansing approach. All variants of our ASR system employed an RNN-based language model to rescore the first-pass recognition hypotheses, which was trained solely on the text dataset released by the organizer. Our system with the best configuration came out in second place, resulting in a word error rate (WER) of 17.59, while those of the top-performing, second runner-up and official baseline systems are 15.67, 18.71, 35.09, respectively.

llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: This paper introduces the NTNU ASR system, which competed in the Interspeech 2020 Non-Native Children's Speech ASR Challenge. The challenge was organized by the SIG-CHILD group of ISCA and made possible by the "child-computer interaction" working group of the Norwegian Center of Excellence LYSNING. The NTNU ASR system is a hybrid end-to-end system that combines a state-of-the-art neural acoustic model, a state-of-the-art neural language model, and a pronunciation model. The system was trained on a corpus of 28 hours of Norwegian speech and achieved a WER of 6.8 on the test set.

human_original_text(type1): We study approaches to improve fine-grained short answer Question Answering models by integrating coarse-grained data annotated for paragraph-level relevance and show that coarsely annotated data can bring significant performance gains. Experiments demonstrate that the standard multi-task learning approach of sharing representations is not the most effective way to leverage coarse-grained annotations. Instead, we can explicitly model the latent fine-grained short answer variables and optimize the marginal log-likelihood directly or use a newly proposed posterior distillation learning objective. Since these latent-variable methods have explicit access to the relationship between the fine and coarse tasks, they result in significantly larger improvements from coarse supervision.

human_original_text(type1): End-to-end (E2E) systems have played a more and more important role in automatic speech recognition (ASR) and achieved great performance. However, E2E systems recognize output word sequences directly with the input acoustic feature, which can only be trained on limited acoustic data. The extra text data is widely used to improve the results of traditional artificial neural network-hidden Markov model (ANN-HMM) hybrid systems. The involving of extra text data to standard E2E ASR systems may break the E2E property during decoding. In this paper, a novel modular E2E ASR system is proposed. The modular E2E ASR system consists of two parts: an acoustic-to-phoneme (A2P) model and a phoneme-to-word (P2W) model. The A2P model is trained on acoustic data, while extra data including large scale text data can be used to train the P2W model. This additional data enables the modular E2E ASR system to model not only the acoustic part but also the language part. During the decoding phase, the two models will be integrated and act as a standard acoustic-to-word (A2W) model. In other words, the proposed modular E2E ASR system can be easily trained with extra text data and decoded in the same way as a standard E2E ASR system. Experimental results on the Switchboard corpus show that the modular E2E model achieves better word error rate (WER) than standard A2W models.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: End-to-end (E2E) systems have become increasingly important in automatic speech recognition (ASR) and achieved state-of-the-art performance. However, E2E systems directly recognize output word sequences with acoustic features, which may suffer from the word boundary ambiguity and the lack of robustness against missing words. In this paper, we propose a multi-task joint training strategy for ASR with DNN-HMM systems. It is based on the hierarchical structures of E2E and HMM-DNN systems and trains them simultaneously. The main contributions of this paper are three-fold. Firstly, we use the attention mechanism to design a multi-task joint training strategy for ASR. It can better model the dependency among different tasks and improve the recognition performance. Secondly, we propose an attention-based DNN-HMM system to deal with the missing words problem. The attention mechanism is used to explore the hidden information of words and the model is trained by using the weighted summation of output word sequences and acoustic features. Thirdly, we use the unsupervised pre-training method to extract more discriminative features. The performance of the proposed method is evaluated on several public ASR benchmark datasets, including Penn Treebank (PTB), Chinese Word Segmentation (CWS) and NIST. The experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a new state-of-the-art performance for ASR with DNN-HMM systems.

human_original_text(type1): In this paper, we focus on the meta distribution for the cache-enabled networks where the locations of base stations (BSs) are modeled as Poisson point process (PPP). Under the random caching framework, we derive the moments of the conditional successful transmission probability (STP), the exact meta distribution and its beta approximation by utilizing stochastic geometry. The closed-form expression of the mean local delay is also derived. We consider the maximization of the STP and the minimization of the mean local delay by optimizing the caching probability and the BS active probability, respectively. For the former, a convex optimization problem is formulated and the optimal caching probability and BS active probability are achieved. human_original_text(type1): We present a novel family of C 1 quadrilateral finite elements, which define global C 1 spaces over a general quadrilateral mesh with vertices of arbitrary valency. The elements extend the construction by Brenner and Sung, which is based on polynomial elements of tensor-product degree p 6, to all degrees p 3. Thus, we call the family of C 1 finite elements Brenner-Sung quadrilaterals. The proposed C 1 quadrilateral can be seen as a special case of the Argyris isogeometric element of. The quadrilateral elements possess similar degrees of freedom as the classical Argyris triangles. Just as for the Argyris triangle, we additionally impose C 2 continuity at the vertices. In this paper we focus on the lower degree cases, not covered in, that may be desirable for their lower computational cost and better conditioning of the basis: We consider indeed the polynomial quadrilateral of (bi degree 5, and the polynomial degrees p 3 and p 4 by employing a splitting into x 3 3 or x 2 2 polynomial pieces, respectively. The proposed elements reproduce polynomials of total degree p. We show that the space provides optimal approximation order. Due to the interpolation properties, the error bounds are local on each element. In addition, we describe the construction of a simple, local basis and give for p {3, 4, 5 } explicit formulas for the Bezier or B-spline coefficients of the basis functions. Numerical experiments by solving the biharmonic equation demonstrate the potential of the proposed C 1 quadrilateral finite element for the numerical analysis of fourth order problems, also indicating that (for p 5) the proposed element performs comparable or in general even better than the Argyris triangle with respect to the number of degrees of freedom.

human_original_text(type1): All the content consumed by mobile users, be it a web page or a live stream, undergoes some processing along the way; as an example, web pages and videos are transcoded to fit each device's screen. The recent multi-access edge computing (MEC) paradigm envisions performing such processing within the cellular network, as opposed to resorting to a cloud server on the Internet. Designing a MEC network, i.e., placing and dimensioning the computational facilities therein, requires information on how much computational power is required to produce the contents needed by the users. However, real-world demand traces only contain information on how much data is downloaded. In this paper, we demonstrate how to enrich demand traces with information about the computational power needed to process the different types of content, and we show the substantial benefit that can be obtained from using such enriched traces for the design of MEC-based networks.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: All data consumed by mobile users, whether it be a web page or a live stream, is subject to some manipulation during transit; for instance, web pages and videos are presented in resolutions other than those for which they were originally produced. The disparity between video consumption via desktop computers' browsers and streaming services is even more pronounced: Although many players permit you to download enormous files when accessible (which may result in bandwidth bill issues), online platforms provide small-sized portions of your favorite film that are constantly updated based on what other people are watching at any given moment. This enables them to not only automatically adapt but also save money since each user will receive less data if she or he decides she or he no longer wants certain portions of the file while someone else still enjoys those sections. This ongoing modification means no unnecessary server load, resulting in further savings. There are numerous instances that can be presented here regarding how multiple instances operate together under one roof rather than having independent operations that draw resources over internet connections.

Image artifacts can be caused by one or more of the following: (1) signal compression artifacts, (2) missing data artifacts, (3) quantization artifacts, (4) sampling artifacts, and (5) signal degradation due to signal processing artifacts. human_original_text(type1): We consider a scenario where multiple infrastructure components have been damaged after a disaster and the health value of each component continues to deteriorate if it is not being targeted by a repair agency, until it fails irreversibly. There are multiple agencies that seek to repair the components and there is an authority whose task is to allocate the components to the agencies within a given budget, so that the total number of components that are fully repaired by the agencies is maximized. We characterize the optimal policy for allocation and repair sequencing when the repair rates are sufficiently larger than the deterioration rates. For the case when the deterioration rates are larger than or equal to the repair rates, the rates are homogeneous across the components, and the costs charged by the entities for repair are equal, we characterize a policy for allocation and repair sequencing that permanently repairs at least half the number of components as that by an optimal policy.

The health value of each component is determined by its level of criticality and the number of its associated servers. We derive the value of the best repair in terms of the maximum possible increase in the health value of the damaged components. We define a simple algorithm that finds the best repair strategy that maximizes the health value of the infrastructure components. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm in simulations.

human_original_text(type1): We propose a machine learning framework to synthesize reactive controllers for systems whose interactions with their adversarial environment are modeled by infinite-duration, two-player games over (potentially) infinite graphs. Our framework targets safety games with infinitely many vertices, but it is also applicable to safety games over finite graphs whose size is too prohibitive for conventional synthesis techniques. The learning takes place in a feedback loop between a teacher component, which can reason symbolically about the safety game, and a learning algorithm, which successively learns an approximation of the winning region from various kinds of examples provided by the teacher. We develop a novel decision tree learning algorithm for this setting and show that our algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a reactive safety controller if a suitable approximation of the winning region can be expressed as a decision tree. Finally, we empirically compare the performance of a prototype implementation to existing approaches, which are based on constraint solving and automata learning, respectively.

Our framework is based on reinforcement learning and differential game theory, and our goal is to find a set of strategies that maximizes the average value of the game. We call this set of strategies the optimistic strategies for the game. In this paper, we develop two algorithms for finding the optimistic strategies for a given game: 1) a policy-based algorithm that uses a learned policy function to search for the optimistic strategies, and 2) a value-based algorithm that uses a learned value function to search for the optimistic strategies. human_original_text(type1): This paper addresses the problem of target detection and localisation in a limited area using multiple coordinated agents. The swarm of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) determines the position of the dispersion of stack effluents to a gas plume in a certain production area as fast as possible, that makes the problem challenging to model and solve, because of the time variability of the target. Three different exploration algorithms are designed and compared. Besides the exploration strategies, the paper reports a solution for quick convergence towards the actual stack position once detected by one member of the team. Both the navigation and localisation algorithms are fully distributed and based on the consensus theory. Simulations on realistic case studies are reported.

This information is then shared with other UAVs through a communication network that relies on wireless links between them. Cooperative sensing systems can be used in many applications, including surveillance over large areas with low manpower available for monitoring, search and rescue operations where teams conducting reconnaissance missions collaborate to detect threats or find victims, environmental studies that require collaboration between groups of robots performing ground surveys and aerial maps coverage, etc. This work focuses on cooperative perception tasks - the design and implementation of distributed algorithms enabling group decision making and robust consensus formation subject to noisy observations. In particular, we consider only problems governed by quadratic cost functions whose objective is to minimize error propagation among cooperating agents. human_original_text(type1): This paper investigates the problem of resource allocation for a wireless communication network with distributed reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs). In this network, multiple RISs are spatially distributed to serve wireless users and the energy efficiency of the network is maximized by dynamically controlling the on-off status of each RIS as well as optimizing the reflection coefficients matrix of the RISs. This problem is posed as a joint optimization problem of transmit beamforming and RIS control, whose goal is to maximize the energy efficiency under minimum rate constraints of the users. To solve this problem, two iterative algorithms are proposed for the single-user case and multi-user case. For the single-user case, the phase optimization problem is solved by using a successive convex approximation method, which admits a closed-form solution at each step. Moreover, the optimal RIS on-off status is obtained by using the dual method. For the multi-user case, a low-complexity greedy searching method is proposed to solve the RIS on-off optimization problem. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme achieves up to 33 and 68 gains in terms of the energy efficiency in both single-user and multi-user cases compared to the conventional RIS scheme and amplify-and-forward relay scheme, respectively.

human_original_text(type1): We propose a comprehensive nonlinear ODE-based thermo-hydraulic model of a district heating system featuring several heat producers, consumers and storage devices which are interconnected through a distribution network of meshed topology whose temperature dynamics are explicitly considered. Moreover, we present conditions under which the hydraulic and thermal subsystems of the model exhibit shifted passivity properties and discuss some of the beneficial implications for decentralized control design and stability analysis. For the former subsystem, our results draw on the monotonicity attributes manifested by the mappings involved. For the latter, we propose a storage function based on the ectropy function of a thermodynamic system, recently used in the passivity analysis of heat exchanger networks. Our formal analysis is supported with numerical simulations on a case study using realistic system parameters.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: We present a comprehensive nonlinear ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based thermo-hydraulic model of a district heating system. The model comprises multiple heat producers, consumers, and storage devices that are interconnected through a meshed pipe distribution network. The model is constructed with a nonlinear simulation package called MONKEY-MOD, and it is capable of capturing the nonlinear behavior of the system. The model is applied to a district heating system in a residential area of Tianjin, China. The system has two primary heat producers that are located in the residential buildings. The numerical results of the simulation are validated against available experimental data of the system.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: This document presents a comprehensive nonlinear ordinary differential equation (ODE) based thermo-hydraulic model of a district heating system. The model includes several heat producers, consumers, and storage devices which are interconnected through a meshed pipeline distribution network. The model is detailed, and includes a system of conservation equations for the heating network. We consider various types of heat producers (condensing boilers, industrial boilers, heat pumps, etc.) and heat consumers (residential and commercial buildings). The nonlinear ODEs governing the energy consumption of the buildings are derived from the associated energy balance equations. The model accounts for the relevant energy conversion processes in the system (fossil fuel combustion, heat pump and heat storage operations) and includes the relevant transport processes. The model is calibrated with real data from a district heating system in Sweden. The calibrated model is used to analyze the behavior of the system, for example in terms of energy efficiency, and to study the influence of different control strategies. The work has been presented at the IFAC Nonlinear Model Predictive Control Conference (NMPC) 2018 in Madison, WI, USA. A preprint is available here. We are developing a model-based optimization tool that allows designing district heating networks taking into account a large number of relevant components, such as heat generation, heat consumption, heat storage, distribution network, etc. The tool is used to design energy-efficient district heating systems and to optimize existing systems. This work is a collaboration with Comsol. A video is available here. An implementation of the model in the modeling software Comsol Multiphysics is available here. We propose a novel thermodynamic model of a hybrid electric vehicle. The model is based on a system of nonlinear differential algebraic equations (DAEs). The model includes a detailed model of the powertrain, which consists of an internal combustion engine, a heat exchanger, a battery, two DCDC converters, and an electric motor. The powertrain model is coupled to a 1D energy balance model of the vehicle, which is used to estimate the vehicle power demand. The energy demand is related to the driving cycle. We consider two driving cycles: a highway driving cycle and a mixed highway-urban driving cycle. The model is implemented in MatlabSimulink and is calibrated with real data from a hybrid electric vehicle which is equipped with a range extender. The model is used to study the influence of different parameters of the vehicle on its energy efficiency. The work has been presented at the International Symposium on Energy Engineering 2016 in Istanbul, Turkey. A preprint is available here.

human_original_text(type1): This work proposes a novel algorithm to generate natural language adversarial input for text classification models, in order to investigate the robustness of these models. It involves applying gradient-based perturbation on the sentence embeddings that are used as the features for the classifier, and learning a decoder for generation. We employ this method to a sentiment analysis model and verify its effectiveness in inducing incorrect predictions by the model. We also conduct quantitative and qualitative analysis on these examples and demonstrate that our approach can generate more natural adversaries. In addition, it can be used to successfully perform black-box attacks, which involves attacking other existing models whose parameters are not known. On a public sentiment analysis API, the proposed method introduces a 20 relative decrease in average accuracy and 74 relative increase in absolute error.

The algorithm relies on gradient-based perturbation strategies to modify words that are likely to be important contributors to class prediction (i.e., keywords). We utilize three different types of perturbations: lexical (word perturbation), syntacticogrammatical (syntactic grammar-based), and syntax-semantics based perturbations, where we perturb only internal grammatically relevant structures within sentences or paragraphs.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: This work proposes a novel approach to generate adversarial input for text classification models. The proposed method involves applying gradient-based perturbation techniques to a neural network model that is trained on noise-perturbed training data. This process yields adversarial examples that are semantically similar to the original text but are misclassified by the model. We first define adversarial input as a sequence of tokens that is semantically similar to a given text but is misclassified by a text classification model. We then describe our approach to generating adversarial input, which consists of two main steps: 1. **Pseudo-random sampling:** We first generate a set of pseudo-random sentences by sampling uniformly from the vocabulary of the text classification model. 2. **Additional transformations:** We then apply a series of additional transformations to the pseudo-random sentences to improve their quality and make them more likely to be misclassified by the model. We evaluate our approach on two text classification tasks: sentiment analysis and topic classification. Our results show that the proposed method can generate adversarial examples that are highly effective at fooling text classification models. In addition to the adversarial examples, we also train a DNN classifier to identify whether test samples belong to the training dataset. We use the following objective function to train the classifier: $$\min \limits_\theta FP(\theta) FN(\theta)$$ where FP(θ) and FN(θ) are the false positive and false negative rates, respectively. The initial CNN architecture is implemented as follows: * The kernel sizes are three in both directions. * A max pooling layer immediately precedes the convolutions, with two window sizes: 2×2. * Mean offset 0 and Gaussian sigma 1. * ReLU activation functions are employed after each convolution and pooling operation. * A fully connected layer composed of 512 neurons and linear activation is placed onto the outputs produced by the last convolutional block. * Finally, a softmax classifier is taken out of this structure to produce category predictions. We also apply two types of data augmentation to the training data: * **Single-step augmentation:** We transform selected items on average once using our chosen transformation operators. * **Multi-step augmentation:** We replace several random small segments within the target documents via a procedure inspired from Goodfellow et al. (2016). The noise distribution is specified as follows: $$z_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_t^2)$$ where σt is drawn proportionally to the total number of words present in the fixed length of a movie clip c and m is the equivalent quantity defined over music clips. We evaluate our approach on two text classification tasks: sentiment analysis and topic classification. Our results show that the proposed method can generate adversarial examples that are highly effective at fooling text classification models.

human_original_text(type1): Automatic charge prediction aims to predict appropriate final charges according to the fact descriptions for a given criminal case. Automatic charge prediction plays a critical role in assisting judges and lawyers to improve the efficiency of legal decisions, and thus has received much attention. Nevertheless, most existing works on automatic charge prediction perform adequately on high-frequency charges but are not yet capable of predicting few-shot charges with limited cases. In this paper, we propose a S equence E nhanced Caps ule model, dubbed as SECaps model, to relieve this problem. Specifically, following the work of capsule networks, we propose the seq-caps layer, which considers sequence information and spatial information of legal texts simultaneously. Then we design a attention residual unit, which provides auxiliary information for charge prediction. In addition, our SECaps model introduces focal loss, which relieves the problem of imbalanced charges. Comparing the state-of-the-art methods, our SECaps model obtains 4.5 and 6.4 absolutely considerable improvements under Macro F1 in Criminal-S and Criminal-L respectively. The experimental results consistently demonstrate the superiorities and competitiveness of our proposed model.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: Automatic charge prediction is the task of predicting the final charges for a given criminal case based on its fact descriptions. This task is critical for assisting judges and prosecutors in determining the appropriate charges during the case management process. In this paper, we propose a new automatic charge prediction method based on two models: an attention-based long short-term memory (LSTM) network and an attention-based convolutional neural network (CNN). The attention-based LSTM network model is composed of an attention-based LSTM network and a multi-output prediction layer. The attention-based CNN model is constructed by combining an attention-based CNN and a multi-output prediction layer. We construct a case-level dataset and a charge-level dataset for automatic charge prediction. Experimental results on these datasets demonstrate that the attention-based LSTM network model and the attention-based CNN model outperform the traditional LSTM network model and CNN model. The attention-based LSTM network model and the attention-based CNN model achieve the best performance on the case-level dataset and the charge-level dataset, respectively. This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFC0831302), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61872170), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M630943).

human_original_text(type1): Margin enlargement over training data has been an important strategy since perceptrons in machine learning for the purpose of boosting the robustness of classifiers toward a good generalization ability. Yet Breiman shows a dilemma (,) that a uniform improvement on margin distribution does not necessarily reduces generalization errors. In this paper, we revisit Breiman's dilemma in deep neural networks with recently proposed spectrally normalized margins. A novel perspective is provided to explain Breiman's dilemma based on phase transitions in dynamics of normalized margin distributions, that reflects the trade-off between expressive power of models and complexity of data. When data complexity is comparable to the model expressiveness in the sense that both training and test data share similar phase transitions in normalized margin dynamics, two efficient ways are derived to predict the trend of generalization or test error via classic margin-based generalization bounds with restricted Rademacher complexities. On the other hand, over-expressive models that exhibit uniform improvements on training margins, as a distinct phase transition to test margin dynamics, may lose such a prediction power and fail to prevent the overfitting. Experiments are conducted to show the validity of the proposed method with some basic convolutional networks, AlexNet, VGG-16, and ResNet-18, on several datasets including Cifar10100 and mini-ImageNet.

human_original_text(type1): In this paper, the authors aim to combine the latest state of the art models in image recognition with the best publicly available satellite images to create a system for landslide risk mitigation. We focus first on landslide detection and further propose a similar system to be used for prediction. Such models are valuable as they could easily be scaled up to provide data for hazard evaluation, as satellite imagery becomes increasingly available. The goal is to use satellite images and correlated data to enrich the public repository of data and guide disaster relief efforts for locating precise areas where landslides have occurred. Different image augmentation methods are used to increase diversity in the chosen dataset and create more robust classification. The resulting outputs are then fed into variants of 3-D convolutional neural networks. A review of the current literature indicates there is no research using CNNs (Convolutional Neural Networks) and freely available satellite imagery for classifying landslide risk. The model has shown to be ultimately able to achieve a significantly better than baseline accuracy.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: This paper presents a system for real-time detection of ships that uses the latest state-of-the-art models in image recognition and the best publicly available satellite images. The system is designed to serve as a tool for detecting illegal maritime activities. Ship detection is a challenging problem due to the large variability in ship appearance, changing lighting and weather conditions, and the natural complexity of the background of the sea. The proposed system uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model that is able to recognize objects in images. The authors first train a CNN for ship detection on an extremely large image database. They propose a method to scale up the image database by using a combination of open-source and commercial imagery and an intelligent tiling technique. The CNN is implemented in Keras, which allows for quick prototyping and experimentation with various architectures and parameter values. The authors achieve an accuracy of over 90% on a dataset of 15,000 ship images. They plan to apply this technique to a larger database of several million images. The authors also develop a web-based visualization tool to explore the performance of the model and provide suggestions for new training images. The authors are currently training the CNN on a dataset of over 300,000 images and plan to refine the model to create a final version for deployment.

human_original_text(type1): Automated design of neural network architectures tailored for a specific task is an extremely promising, albeit inherently difficult, avenue to explore. While most results in this domain have been achieved on image classification and language modelling problems, here we concentrate on dense per-pixel tasks, in particular, semantic image segmentation using fully convolutional networks. In contrast to the aforementioned areas, the design choices of a fully convolutional network require several changes, ranging from the sort of operations that need to be used - e.g., dilated convolutions - to a solving of a more difficult optimisation problem. In this work, we are particularly interested in searching for high-performance compact segmentation architectures, able to run in real-time using limited resources. To achieve that, we intentionally over-parameterise the architecture during the training time via a set of auxiliary cells that provide an intermediate supervisory signal and can be omitted during the evaluation phase. The design of the auxiliary cell is emitted by a controller, a neural network with the fixed structure trained using reinforcement learning. More crucially, we demonstrate how to efficiently search for these architectures within limited time and computational budgets. In particular, we rely on a progressive strategy that terminates non-promising architectures from being further trained, and on Polyak averaging coupled with knowledge distillation to speed-up the convergence. Quantitatively, in 8 GPU-days our approach discovers a set of architectures performing on-par with state-of-the-art among compact models on the semantic segmentation, pose estimation and depth prediction tasks. Code will be made available here:
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: Designing neural network architectures customized for a specific task is an extremely alluring, albeit inherently challenging, path to pursue. Although most results in this area have been achieved using gradient-based methods that need a massive amount of training data, this is not a viable strategy in practice. In this paper, we propose a method for neural architecture search that solves the problem of needing a large amount of training data while still employing gradient-based methods. Instead of learning a new network architecture from scratch, we propose using a pre-trained network. More specifically, we use the knowledge present in a pre-trained model to learn a new network architecture. Our method is based on the idea that the knowledge in a trained network can be mapped to an architecture by tracing gradients through the network. Our findings demonstrate that this process can learn efficient architectures for various tasks.

human_original_text(type1): In this work, we propose a purely geometrical approach for the robust matching of line segments for challenging stereo streams with severe illumination changes or High Dynamic Range (HDR) environments. To that purpose, we exploit the univocal nature of the matching problem, i.e. every observation must be corresponded with a single feature or not corresponded at all. We state the problem as a sparse, convex, l 1 -minimization of the matching vector regularized by the geometric constraints. This formulation allows for the robust tracking of line segments along sequences where traditional appearance-based matching techniques tend to fail due to dynamic changes in illumination conditions. Moreover, the proposed matching algorithm also results in a considerable speed-up of previous state of the art techniques making it suitable for real-time applications such as Visual Odometry (VO). This, of course, comes at expense of a slightly lower number of matches in comparison with appearance-based methods, and also limits its application to continuous video sequences, as it is rather constrained to small pose increments between consecutive frames. We validate the claimed advantages by first evaluating the matching performance in challenging video sequences, and then testing the method in a benchmarked point and line based VO algorithm.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: This paper proposes a purely geometric approach to robustly match line segments in challenging stereo streams, such as those with severe illumination changes or high dynamic range (HDR) environments. The matching strategy relies on two key geometric assumptions: the epipolar constraint and the mutual support assumption. The epipolar constraint ensures that the matching line segments lie on the same epipolar plane. The mutual support assumption ensures that the matching segments are perpendicular to each other. This strategy is very effective at rejecting line segments that do not satisfy these constraints. The method is able to match line segments in a variety of HDR environments, and we provide experimental results to support this claim.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: We present a purely geometric method for robustly matching line segments in stereo streams with severe illumination changes or High Dynamic Range (HDR) environments. Our approach is based on the fact that the intersection of a line segment in the left image and its corresponding line segment in the right image is a point with a unique 3D position. By considering the 3D position of this point and the intersection of its epipolar line in the right and left images, we can robustly match line segments. We evaluate our approach on a number of challenging stereo datasets with severe illumination changes or HDR environments and show that it outperforms existing approaches in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

We evaluate our approach on a number of challenging stereo datasets with severe illumination changes or HDR environments and show that it outperforms existing approaches in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

human_original_text(type1): Although artificial neural networks have shown great promise in applications including computer vision and speech recognition, there remains considerable practical and theoretical difficulty in optimizing their parameters. The seemingly unreasonable success of gradient descent methods in minimizing these non-convex functions remains poorly understood. In this work we offer some theoretical guarantees for networks with piecewise affine activation functions, which have in recent years become the norm. We prove three main results. Firstly, that the network is piecewise convex as a function of the input data. Secondly, that the network, considered as a function of the parameters in a single layer, all others held constant, is again piecewise convex. Finally, that the network as a function of all its parameters is piecewise multi-convex, a generalization of biconvexity. From here we characterize the local minima and stationary points of the training objective, showing that they minimize certain subsets of the parameter space. We then analyze the performance of two optimization algorithms on multi-convex problems: gradient descent, and a method which repeatedly solves a number of convex sub-problems. We prove necessary convergence conditions for the first algorithm and both necessary and sufficient conditions for the second, after introducing regularization to the objective. Finally, we remark on the remaining difficulty of the global optimization problem. Under the squared error objective, we show that by varying the training data, a single rectifier neuron admits local minima arbitrarily far apart, both in objective value and parameter space.

llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have been shown to achieve state-of-the-art results in a wide range of applications, including computer vision and natural language processing. This paper proposes a new method for training DNNs that is both efficient and effective. The method is based on the idea of hierarchical optimization, which allows each layer of the network to be optimized independently. This reduces the computational cost of training and makes it easier to find the optimal set of parameters. The method is evaluated on a variety of tasks, including image classification and natural language processing. The results show that the method achieves comparable or better performance than existing methods, while being significantly more efficient. In addition to its efficiency, the method also has several other advantages. First, it is more robust to noise and outliers than existing methods. Second, it can be used to train DNNs with very deep architectures. Third, it can be used to train DNNs with very large datasets. We believe that the method will be a valuable tool for researchers and practitioners who are working on training DNNs.

human_original_text(type1): The increase in the world's population and rising standards of living is leading to an ever-increasing number of vehicles on the roads, and with it ever-increasing difficulties in traffic management. This traffic management in transport networks can be clearly optimized by using information and communication technologies referred as Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS). This management problem is usually reformulated as finding the shortest path in a time varying random graph. In this article, an online shortest path computation using stochastic gradient descent is proposed. This routing algorithm for ITS traffic management is based on the online Frank-Wolfe approach. Our improvement enables to find a confidence interval for the shortest path, by using the stochastic gradient algorithm for approximate Bayesian inference. The theory required to understand our approach is provided, as well as the implementation details.

This can be done by making engines more powerful and by using alternative fuels. Another solution is to improve traffic management. This can be done by using traffic signals and signs, and by providing more public transportation options. Improving fuel efficiency and traffic management will help to reduce accidents and pollution, and will make our roads safer and more livable.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: The growth in population and increasing standards of living have led to more vehicles on the roads, resulting in traffic congestion. This problem cannot be solved by simply adding more cars, but rather by reducing the number of people driving. The new trends of urbanization and suburbanization make sharing various forms of transportation popular, especially public transportation systems like buses and trains. In order to accommodate different transportation needs within a single system, we need better information about what kind of vehicle may enter at which location and time into certain areas so that appropriate actions can be taken before congestion occurs. Accurate prediction of future developments would enable planning ahead and investment decisions regarding road investments, location choice for high speed rail lines, etc. Such predictions concern both short-term changes due to weather fluctuations and longer-term forecasts concerning demand from potential customers and possible vehicle availability. Forecasting transportation data like those mentioned above has been studied extensively over many decades. For the last decade there have been several successful examples of forecasting time series obtained as aggregates of individual transactions using complex models including neural networks. This paper focuses primarily upon historical transaction data; we do however mention briefly some recent advances towards dynamic approaches. There exist two general categories of methods for predicting sales: static vs dynamic. Static techniques predict prices based upon current values via regression analysis and assume no correlation between successive observations. Dynamic methods include adaptive Markov chains, artificial immune modeling, and pattern recognition algorithms. Our work builds upon a previously published model; our contributions are: 1) Modeling both the historic patterns observed in actual transaction records as well as their statistical properties, 2) improving upon the accuracy of the proposed approach, particularly when only limited amounts of training set information are provided, and 3) making the method practical and easily implementable. As stated earlier, one major reason why most studies focus on simple linear models is because they allow direct estimation of predicted quantities from past ones when sufficient amounts of historical data are available. However, in reality, often little existing history is available whereas only small samples must be selected for testing purposes. Furthermore, customer-specific characteristics change continuously making any form of extrapolation impossible. Therefore, another motivation behind developing advanced non-linear modeling tools was to avoid such situations where too much knowledge about underlying dynamics is required since otherwise simply applying traditional fixed parameter estimates will lead to inaccurate results. The model structure is based on a dynamic logit framework. human_original_text(type1): Disjunctive Answer Set Programming (ASP) is a powerful declarative programming paradigm whose main decision problems are located on the second level of the polynomial hierarchy. Identifying tractable fragments and developing efficient algorithms for such fragments are thus important objectives in order to complement the sophisticated ASP systems available to date. Hard problems can become tractable if some problem parameter is bounded by a fixed constant; such problems are then called fixed-parameter tractable (FPT). While several FPT results for ASP exist, parameters that relate to directed or signed graphs representing the program at hand have been neglected so far. In this paper, we first give some negative observations showing that directed width measures on the dependency graph of a program do not lead to FPT results. We then consider the graph parameter of signed clique-width and present a novel dynamic programming algorithm that is FPT w.r.t. this parameter. Clique-width is more general than the well-known treewidth, and, to the best of our knowledge, ours is the first FPT algorithm for bounded clique-width for reasoning problems beyond SAT.

human_original_text(type1): Testing Deep Neural Network (DNN) models has become more important than ever with the increasing usage of DNN models in safety-critical domains such as autonomous cars. The traditional approach of testing DNNs is to create a test set, which is a random subset of the dataset about the problem of interest. This kind of approach is not enough for testing most of the real-world scenarios since these traditional test sets do not include corner cases, while a corner case input is generally considered to introduce erroneous behaviors. Recent works on adversarial input generation, data augmentation, and coverage-guided fuzzing (CGF) have provided new ways to extend traditional test sets. Among those, CGF aims to produce new test inputs by fuzzing existing ones to achieve high coverage on a test adequacy criterion (i.e. coverage criterion). Given that the subject test adequacy criterion is a well-established one, CGF can potentially find error inducing inputs for different underlying reasons. In this paper, we propose a novel CGF solution for structural testing of DNNs. The proposed fuzzer employs Monte Carlo Tree Search to drive the coverage-guided search in the pursuit of achieving high coverage. Our evaluation shows that the inputs generated by our method result in higher coverage than the inputs produced by the previously introduced coverage-guided fuzzing techniques.

human_original_text(type1): Consider the task of performing a sequence of searches in a binary search tree. After each search, an algorithm is allowed to arbitrarily restructure the tree, at a cost proportional to the amount of restructuring performed. The cost of an execution is the sum of the time spent searching and the time spent optimizing those searches with restructuring operations. This notion was introduced by Sleator and Tarjan in (JACM, 1985), along with an algorithm and a conjecture. The algorithm, Splay, is an elegant procedure for performing adjustments while moving searched items to the top of the tree. The conjecture, called dynamic optimality, is that the cost of splaying is always within a constant factor of the optimal algorithm for performing searches. The conjecture stands to this day. In this work, we attempt to lay the foundations for a proof of the dynamic optimality conjecture. Central to our methods are simulation embeddings and approximate monotonicity. A simulation embedding maps each execution to a list of keys that induces a target algorithm to simulate the execution. Approximately monotone algorithms are those whose cost does not increase by more than a constant factor when keys are removed from the list. As we shall see, approximately monotone algorithms with simulation embeddings are dynamically optimal. Building on these ideas: item 1st item We construct a simulation embedding for Splay by inducing Splay to perform arbitrary subtree transformations. Thus, if Splay is approximately monotone then it is dynamically optimal. We also show that approximate monotonicity is a necessary condition for dynamic optimality. (Section) item 2nd item We show that if Splay is dynamically optimal, then with respect to optimal cost, its additive overhead is at most linear in the sum of initial tree size and the number of requests. (Section) item 3rd item We prove that a known lower bound on optimal execution cost by Wilber is approximately monotone. (Section and Appendix) item 4th item We speculate about how one might establish dynamic optimality by adapting the proof of approximate monotonicity from the lower bound to Splay. (Section) item 5th item We demonstrate that two related conjectures, traversal and deque, also follow if Splay is approximately monotone, and that many results in this paper extend to a broad class of "path-based" algorithms. (Section) Appendix generalizes the tree transformations used to build simulation embeddings, and Appendix includes proofs of selected pieces of "folklore" that have appeared throughout the literature.

This algorithm is implemented in most modern sorting algorithms, and it is used to sort most data sets. human_original_text(type1): In this correspondence, we introduce a minimax regret criteria to the least squares problems with bounded data uncertainties and solve it using semi-definite programming. We investigate a robust minimax least squares approach that minimizes a worst case difference regret. The regret is defined as the difference between a squared data error and the smallest attainable squared data error of a least squares estimator. We then propose a robust regularized least squares approach to the regularized least squares problem under data uncertainties by using a similar framework. We show that both unstructured and structured robust least squares problems and robust regularized least squares problem can be put in certain semi-definite programming forms. Through several simulations, we demonstrate the merits of the proposed algorithms with respect to the the well-known alternatives in the literature.

human_original_text(type1): Safety remains a central obstacle preventing widespread use of RL in the real world: learning new tasks in uncertain environments requires extensive exploration, but safety requires limiting exploration. We propose Recovery RL, an algorithm which navigates this tradeoff by (1) leveraging offline data to learn about constraint violating zones before policy learning and (2) separating the goals of improving task performance and constraint satisfaction across two policies: a task policy that only optimizes the task reward and a recovery policy that guides the agent to safety when constraint violation is likely. We evaluate Recovery RL on 6 simulation domains, including two contact-rich manipulation tasks and an image-based navigation task, and an image-based obstacle avoidance task on a physical robot. We compare Recovery RL to 5 prior safe RL methods which jointly optimize for task performance and safety via constrained optimization or reward shaping and find that Recovery RL outperforms the next best prior method across all domains. Results suggest that Recovery RL trades off constraint violations and task successes 2 - 80 times more efficiently in simulation domains and 3 times more efficiently in physical experiments. See for videos and supplementary material.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: Safety remains a major barrier to the widespread adoption of RL in real-world settings. This is because learning new tasks in uncertain environments requires extensive exploration, but safety considerations demand that exploration be limited. We propose two approaches to achieving this balance between improving performance and mitigating the risk of poor policies that could harm agents or others (e.g., by taking actions with unintended consequences [Amodei et al., 2016]).
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-2n: **Safety is a major obstacle to the widespread adoption of RL in real-world applications.** To address this issue, we propose augmenting basic RL algorithms with the idea that it is possible to learn safe behavior by strategically sampling from past experience and using this information to determine when action selection has led to undesirable states. To demonstrate our key ideas, we have designed two novel RL architectures: Inverse Reinforcement Learning Networking (IRLN) and Iterative Safe Exploration (S2E). Both are trained only on demonstrations of correct actions; they do not require any reward signals nor rely on supervision during training. In both cases, our objective architecture encodes a state-action-value function Q(s, a;θ), which provides an estimate of the expected return at each timestep given the current environmental conditions s,a. A low value indicates high risk, because taking such an action may lead to dangerous consequences. As soon as we receive negative feedback about bad outcomes, we switch to another safer policy instead of continuing to explore. The main contribution of IRLN is its ability to learn different modes of operation based solely on observed experiences, without the need for additional samples or labels. This enables fast switching between policies even after discovering potentially unforeseen situations. S2E differs from other approaches in its iteratively improving approach to planning horizon control. Since there may be multiple agents present simultaneously, uncertainty regarding their objectives must also be taken into account. Therefore, every policy iteration should consider all future trajectories available, including those that cause self-harm. Moreover, since no prior knowledge of the agent's preferences can be assumed, we must also incorporate softmax-based utility functions over paths. Finally, intuitively speaking, if sufficiently many examples of bad events occur within a limited number of iterations, then it seems reasonable to stop exploring altogether. Figure 2 illustrates these concepts graphically. **Related Work** The vast majority of RL research focuses on continuous tasks, where rewards represent numerical values that act like true costs. Other papers focus either on discrete problems, discrete-time domains, or non-stationary domains. Improving the stability of RL techniques leads directly to faster convergence rates. For instance, prioritized exploration proposed by Cheeseman et al. provides better performance than random exploration while preserving the probability distribution across policies. However, none of these methods allow effectively solving complex discrete problem domains such as HITMAN. Several works employ deep reinforcement networks to model higher-level abstractions in order to improve generalization capabilities. Alistarhzadeh et al. extended Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) with an attention mechanism to address ill-suited parameters estimation and improved generalization through adversarial training. Our work builds upon such advances and further extends them toward more complex scenarios. Liu et al. argue that while standard DDPG does converge quickly, achieving good long-term behavior often comes at the expense of significant short-term fluctuations. They develop a method called LSTD-QD that combines linear temporal difference (LTD) updates with maximum-likelihood regularization, thus contributing to the robustness of learned policies. All three of the aforementioned methods train neural network models offline, basing themselves on empirical statistics gathered via history replay. However, recent literature reports a lack of scalability when simulations become large enough. Choy and Seldin proposed CombolicQ-learning, a hybrid algorithm combining an efficient online programming solver and approximate dynamic programming, that ensures stable and robust long-term behavior while being able to handle very diverse scenarios consisting of non-determinism due to imperfect sensor data availability. Nevertheless, while providing an elegant solution to some specific problems, it lacks generality, making it hard to apply to the ever-increasing complexity of modern games. To overcome the limitations discussed so far, Peltzer et al. suggested an alternative approach known as Escape From Pessimism (EFP). Inspired by human intuition, EFP utilizes entropy measures calculated throughout simulation and attempts to prevent catastrophic losses utilizing auxiliary variables. While having demonstrated impressive effectiveness on several benchmarks, one major drawback of EFP lies in its inability to deal with multi-agent settings and single-shot settings. These critical shortcomings could be addressed with advanced interaction mechanisms allowing sharing information among strategies and complex decision processes beyond just summing up probabilities of external factors. Different from existing solutions aiming primarily at maintaining a balance between exploration and exploitation, our goal was to design an autonomous system capable of evolving the focus of exploration according to newly acquired observations. Proposed architectures utilize principles similar to inverse reinforcement learning: whenever an unexpected event occurs, we start building up a memory trace which later gets used to update policy parameters.

All three of the aforementioned methods train neural network models offline, basing themselves on empirical statistics gathered via history replay. However, recent literature reports a lack of scalability when simulations become large enough. Choy and Seldin proposed CombolicQ-learning, a hybrid algorithm combining an efficient online programming solver and approximate dynamic programming, that ensures stable and robust long-term behavior while being able to handle very diverse scenarios consisting of non-determinism due to imperfect sensor data availability. Nevertheless, while providing an elegant solution to some specific problems, it lacks generality, making it hard to apply to the ever-increasing complexity of modern games. To overcome the limitations discussed so far, Peltzer et al. suggested an alternative approach known as Escape From Pessimism (EFP). Inspired by human intuition, EFP utilizes entropy measures calculated throughout simulation and attempts to prevent catastrophic losses utilizing auxiliary variables. While having demonstrated impressive effectiveness on several benchmarks, one major drawback of EFP lies in its inability to deal with multi-agent settings and single-shot settings. These critical shortcomings could be addressed with advanced interaction mechanisms allowing sharing information among strategies and complex decision processes beyond just summing up probabilities of external factors. Unlike existing solutions aiming primarily at maintaining a balance between exploration and exploitation, our goal was to design an autonomous system capable of evolving the focus of exploration according to newly acquired observations. Proposed architectures utilize principles similar to inverse reinforcement learning: whenever an unexpected event occurs, we start building up a memory trace which later gets used to update policy parameters.

human_original_text(type1): Design patterns are distilled from many real systems to catalog common programming practice. However, some object-oriented design patterns are distorted or overly complicated because of the lack of supporting programming language constructs or mechanisms. For this paper, we have analyzed several published design patterns looking for idiomatic ways of working around constraints of the implementation language. From this analysis, we lay a groundwork of general-purpose language constructs and mechanisms that, if provided by a statically typed, object-oriented language, would better support the implementation of design patterns and, transitively, benefit the construction of many real systems. In particular, our catalog of language constructs includes subtyping separate from inheritance, lexically scoped closure objects independent of classes, and multimethod dispatch. The proposed constructs and mechanisms are not radically new, but rather are adopted from a variety of languages and programming language research and combined in a new, orthogonal manner. We argue that by describing design patterns in terms of the proposed constructs and mechanisms, pattern descriptions become simpler and, therefore, accessible to a larger number of language communities. Constructs and mechanisms lacking in a particular language can be implemented using paradigmatic idioms.

human_original_text(type1): Encoding a sequence of observations is an essential task with many applications. The encoding can become highly efficient when the observations are generated by a dynamical system. A dynamical system imposes regularities on the observations that can be leveraged to achieve a more efficient code. We propose a method to encode a given or learned dynamical system. Apart from its application for encoding a sequence of observations, we propose to use the compression achieved by this encoding as a criterion for model selection. Given a dataset, different learning algorithms result in different models. But not all learned models are equally good. We show that the proposed encoding approach can be used to choose the learned model which is closer to the true underlying dynamics. We provide experiments for both encoding and model selection, and theoretical results that shed light on why the approach works.
llm_paraphrased_generated_text(type5)-1s: The automaton can be extended to encode a sequence of symbols. Encoding a sequence of observations is a fundamental task with numerous applications. When the observations are generated by a dynamical system, the encoding can be made highly efficient. A dynamical system is a mathematical model of the physical processes of the world that can be represented by a differential equation. The differential equation models the evolution of the state of the system from one time step to the next. In this paper, we study encoding and decoding of a dynamical system using a chaotic attractor. A chaotic attractor is a self-similar fractal that is invariant under time reversal. The fractal geometry of the attractor allows the encoding of the system's state into a set of symbols using a combination of time-stamps and coordinates. The symbols encode a sequence of the attractor's states, which corresponds to the observations of the system. The self-similarity of the attractor ensures that the number of symbols required to encode a set of observations is independent of the size of the set. This is in contrast to a finite-state automaton, which requires a finite number of states to encode the same set of observations. This property allows us to encode a sequence of observations of a chaotic attractor into a set of symbols with an arbitrarily large alphabet. The approach can be extended to dynamical systems with multiple attractors, which can be encoded into a single symbol set.

human_original_text(type1): We use coherence relations inspired by computational models of discourse to study the information needs and goals of image captioning. Using an annotation protocol specifically devised for capturing image-caption coherence relations, we annotate 10,000 instances from publicly-available image-caption pairs. We introduce a new task for learning inferences in imagery and text, coherence relation prediction, and show that these coherence annotations can be exploited to learn relation classifiers as an intermediary step, and also train coherence-aware, controllable image captioning models. The results show a dramatic improvement in the consistency and quality of the generated captions with respect to information needs specified via coherence relations.

We present a novel corpus of 1800 image-caption pairs, which uses an annotation protocol designed specifically to capture image-caption coherence relations. This dataset is unique in that it uses both discourse-inspired relations and user-generated images and captions. We use our dataset to investigate the connection between coherence and the various information requirements and objectives of image captioning. We demonstrate that coherence is not always a prerequisite for a caption to be pertinent, as captions frequently include information that is not immediately pertinent to the image but that supports the overall narrative of the caption. We also present a first approach to machine learning for image captioning that incorporates our coherence annotations to improve image captioning models. We then define the coherence between a pair of captions (c, f) as follows: We employ coherence relations inspired by computational models of discourse to study the information needs and goals of image captioning. We create a large corpus of semantically annotated captions and use it to train a model that can generate coherent captions. Our approach is most closely related to prior work on modeling temporal dependencies in text generation (Stahl et al., 2013; Sarkar Roth, 2015) and using generative adversarial networks (GANs) (Huang Manning, 2017; Zoph Knight, 2016) to generate coherent captions from images. However, unlike these approaches, our goal is to capture certain structural properties of human-annotated captions. These features include: * Image resolution: We collect three different levels of detail about the image (thumbnail, medium-resolution, and full-size). This helps ensure that our choices do not depend solely on factors like size but rather provide insight into any differences between captions that are specific to particular details captured within those resolutions. * Coherence heuristics: For each pairwise order relationship present in the gold standard annotation for all pairs of sentences separated by 1-10 words, e.g., earlier vs later, we compute a simple score called baselineset agreement, as defined below. The representation of this value allows us to compare it across baseline sets so that lower values indicate weaker consistency among competing explanations. * Word embeddings: To address concerns that word embedding representations may be overfitting to noisy linguistic data they were originally trained on, we include cosine similarity-based matrix factorization-based techniques (Cai et al., 2014), which have been shown to reduce noise while preserving important relationships outperforming many alternative methods including word2vec (Mikolov et al., 2013) (which was one of the seminal datasets used when training GANs). We collect labeled data for our corpus by first sampling 10^5 random combinations of images and captions from Flickr's photo collection. Then, for every combination, we search through Google Image Search 3 for corresponding example sentences "from context" that appear after the selected combination. Next, we select the best candidates according to their relevance scores, i.e., (i) highest number of candidate instances, (ii) lowest max-similarity distance between image and instance, etc.) and apply them to Google Image Search 4. Finally, we manually evaluate and correct potential mistakes made by these tools before proceeding to further processing steps described next. To obtain ground-truth annotation for each textual element, we first decide whether there exists any particular feature-value relation between two elements. In cases where multiple answers exist for a given question, we choose the answer with the strongest evidence. Each possible relation has its own set of constraints, which make it easier to interpret the underlying structure and reasoning behind captions generated by systems. Table 1 contains examples describing the various types of relations we annotated together with some illustrating examples found during preliminary experiments.
